paper.title_en: Node-locked phase of annual modulations from the gravitational chiral anomaly in the solar Kerr field
paper.abstract_en: The Chern-Pontryagin density of the Sun's Kerr field, ${}^*RR\simeq 288\,G^{2}M_{\odot}^{2}a_{\odot}\cosθ/c^{4}r^{7}$, provides an annually sign-changing parity-odd curvature invariant sampled by a detector in the Solar System and sources the gravitational chiral anomaly $\nabla_μj^μ_{5}={}^*RR/384π^{2}$. Sampled along the Earth's orbit it is odd about the solar equatorial plane, so the accumulated chirality is extremal at the plane crossings. Any observable coupled to this reservoir therefore modulates annually with a phase fixed entirely by ephemerides: the cosine-fit phase is $t^{*}=158.7$ d (June 7-8) for a rate-suppressing coupling or $t^{*}=341.4$ d (December 7-8) for a rate-enhancing one, with a calculable secular drift of $+0.014$ d yr$^{-1}$, strict energy independence, a phase-locked semiannual harmonic of $3.75\%$, and no synodic (27 d) or solar-cycle (11 yr) components. The sign and magnitude of the coupling are not predicted; the phase is. A single-amplitude fit to the DAMA/LIBRA-phase2 residuals selects the suppressing branch and describes the observed modulation as well as the standard-halo dark-matter cosine ($Δχ^{2}=1.8$). The most precise published phase, from the full 3.40 t yr exposure, $t^{*}=153.5\pm3.8$ d, lies $0.3σ$ from the halo value and $1.4σ$ from the node-locked one: the $6.2$-day separation between the hypotheses is below the current resolution. Unlike the halo phase, the node-locked phase admits no astrophysical adjustment, so phase metrology at the two-day level, and a test of its energy independence, discriminates between the two hypotheses.
